title: 工具
summary: 字典、字幕、浏览器插件与 AI 辅助工具清单。
body: 字典、字幕、浏览器插件与 AI 辅助工具清单。 本文来自 luckliu2007/English-study 仓库，作为应用内「阅读」材料随仓库同步更新。

title: 主动回忆法
summary: 通过「从记忆中提取」而非反复阅读来强化记忆，是最高效的学习法之一。
body: 主动回忆（active recall）指合上书本，凭记忆把内容写下来或说出来，再对照核对。研究一致表明，它比重复阅读更有效，因为提取动作本身会强化神经通路。用法：读完一节后用空白卡片自测、做完题先回想知识点、用费曼技巧复述。把它嵌入每天复习，长期记忆 retention 会明显提升。

title: 费曼技巧
summary: 用「教给小白」的方式检验是否真懂，逼出知识盲区。
body: 费曼技巧四步：① 选一个概念，假装在教一个外行；② 用最简单的话写下来，遇到卡壳就是盲区；③ 回看资料补齐盲区；④ 再简化类比。它能把「以为懂」变成「真懂」，特别适合语法、发音规则与学术概念。每周挑一个难点走一遍，理解深度会快速收敛。

title: 番茄工作法
summary: 25 分钟专注 + 5 分钟休息的节奏，对抗拖延、保住注意力。
body: 番茄工作法把学习切成 25 分钟一段（一个「番茄」），段间休息 5 分钟，每 4 段长休 15–30 分钟。它降低启动门槛、减少手机干扰，并让进度可见。配合每日 20–35 分钟的学习任务正好一个番茄，完成后打勾记录，坚持感和成就感会随之累积。

title: 康奈尔笔记法
summary: 一页三分栏：线索 / 笔记 / 总结，复习时遮住笔记自测。
body: 康奈尔笔记把一页分为三区：右侧记要点，左侧写关键词/问题线索，底部写一句话总结。复习时遮住右侧，只看左侧线索回忆内容——本质仍是主动回忆。它结构清晰、便于考前速览，适合听课、读材料与看视频时同步使用。

title: 交错练习
summary: 混合不同题型/技能交替练，比长时间单刷一种更利于迁移。
body: 交错练习（interleaving）指把相近技能打乱顺序练，例如听力、口语、语法轮换，而非连续两小时只练听力。虽当下更吃力，但长期迁移与辨别力更强。做法上，可以把当天的薄弱技能主动插进学习计划里轮换，天然形成交错。

title: 元认知
summary: 「对思考的思考」：计划—监控—复盘，让学习可被自己调控。
body: 元认知是检视自己怎么学、学得怎样的能力，分三步：计划（我要达到什么、用什么策略）、监控（我走神了吗、这方法有效吗）、复盘（哪错、为什么、下次怎么改）。把每次练习的得分和各项反馈记录下来，对照目标调整下一步，就是在把元认知外显化。

title: 学习小组
summary: 同伴讲解、互相纠错、责任共担，口语与坚持都更稳。
body: 学习小组用「教别人」巩固自己，并在低风险环境练口语、互相纠错。3–5 人最佳，固定时间、带明确目标（如每周一个测验 + 一段 shadowing）。配合应用内学习路径，可把小组任务拆成每日可完成的微步骤，避免「组会变闲聊」。

title: 考试焦虑应对
summary: 用模拟、呼吸与重构认知把焦虑降到「适度唤醒」区间。
body: 考试焦虑源于不确定感与灾难化想象。对策：① 模拟考——按真实时长限时做，降低陌生感；② 盒式呼吸（4-4-4-4）在考前 1 分钟平复；③ 把「我会考砸」重构为「我已按计划练过，正常发挥即可」。每周做一次低压力的常态化模拟测验，也能显著降低临场紧张。

title: My Morning
summary: 用最简单的短句描述一个普通早晨的日常。
body: Every morning I wake up at seven o'clock. First, I open the window and look at the sky. Then I brush my teeth and wash my face. I eat bread and eggs for breakfast. I drink a cup of milk too. My sister makes tea. After breakfast, I put on my shoes and take my bag. I walk to the bus stop with my friend. The bus comes at eight. We talk and laugh on the bus. I like my mornings. They are simple and happy.

questions.q: What time does the writer wake up?
How does the writer go to the bus stop?

title: My Cat Mimi
summary: 一篇关于宠物猫日常的入门级短文。
body: I have a small cat. Her name is Mimi. She is white and grey. Mimi likes to sleep on my bed all day. In the morning, she is hungry. She looks at me and says meow. I give her food and water. She eats fast. Then she plays with a ball. Sometimes she runs after her tail. It is very funny. At night, Mimi sits on my legs. She is warm and soft. I love my cat very much. She is my best friend at home.

questions.q: What colour is Mimi?
When is Mimi hungry?
Where does Mimi like to sleep?

title: A Trip to the Mountains
summary: 一次全家坐火车去山间小镇的短途旅行记。
body: Last summer, my family took a train to a small town near the mountains. The trip was five hours long, but we did not feel bored. We looked out of the window and saw green fields, rivers, and old bridges. When we arrived, the air was fresh and cool. We stayed in a small hotel next to a lake. Every morning we walked around the water and took photos. In the afternoon, we tried local food. My favourite was a warm soup with mushrooms. On the last day, it started to rain, so we drank tea and played cards inside. I hope we can go back next year.

questions.q: How long was the train trip?
What did the family do on the last day?
options: They drank tea and played cards

questions.q: Where was the hotel?

title: Cooking at Home
summary: 介绍在家做饭为什么简单、便宜又健康。
body: Cooking at home is easier than many people think. You do not need many tools or expensive food. To start, choose a simple dish, like fried rice or noodle soup. Wash your vegetables and cut them into small pieces. Heat some oil in a pan, and add garlic first because it gives a nice smell. Then put in your vegetables and cook them for a few minutes. Add rice, salt, and a little soy sauce. Mix everything well. In about fifteen minutes, your meal is ready. Cooking your own food is cheaper, and it is often healthier too. Best of all, you can make it exactly the way you like.

questions.q: What should you add to the pan first?
According to the text, cooking at home is NOT:

title: Living with Smartphones
summary: 智能手机如何改变生活，以及便利背后的代价。
body: Smartphones have changed the way we live in only a few decades. Twenty years ago, a phone was mainly used to make calls and send short messages. Today, the same small device can take photos, play music, guide us through unknown cities, and connect us to people on the other side of the world. For many students, a smartphone is also a powerful learning tool. With the right apps, you can practise a new language, read the news, or study for an exam while waiting for the bus. However, this convenience comes with a cost. Because phones are always with us, it can be hard to focus or to rest. Some people check their screens hundreds of times a day without even noticing. The key is balance: using technology as a tool, not letting it use us.

questions.q: How were phones mainly used twenty years ago?
options: To make calls and send messages

questions.q: What problem does the text mention?
options: It can be hard to focus or rest

questions.q: What does the writer suggest at the end?

title: Why Recycling Matters
summary: 回收如何帮助地球，以及为什么减少消费更重要。
body: Every year, cities around the world produce millions of tonnes of waste. A large part of this rubbish could be reused or recycled, yet much of it still ends up in landfills or in the ocean. Recycling is one simple way that ordinary people can help the planet. Paper, glass, metal, and many types of plastic can be collected, cleaned, and turned into new products. This process saves energy and reduces the need to cut down trees or dig up raw materials. Still, recycling alone is not enough. Experts say the most important step is to reduce how much we buy and throw away in the first place. Carrying a reusable bag, choosing products with less packaging, and repairing broken items instead of replacing them all make a real difference over time.

questions.q: According to experts, what is the most important step?
options: Reducing how much we buy and throw away

questions.q: What does recycling save?

title: The Rise of Remote Work
summary: 远程办公的优势、隐性挑战与混合办公趋势。
body: Over the past few years, remote work has shifted from a rare privilege to a common arrangement in many industries. Employees who once spent hours commuting now open their laptops at home and join meetings from their living rooms. Supporters argue that this flexibility improves both productivity and quality of life. Without a daily commute, people gain time for family, exercise, or rest, and companies can hire talent regardless of location. Yet remote work also brings subtle challenges. Casual conversations by the coffee machine, which often spark new ideas, are harder to replicate on a screen. New employees may struggle to build relationships or absorb the unspoken culture of a team. Some workers report that the boundary between their job and personal life has almost disappeared, leaving them tired and unable to switch off. Increasingly, organisations are turning to hybrid models, blending office days with remote ones, in the hope of capturing the benefits of both while limiting their drawbacks.

questions.q: What is one benefit of remote work mentioned in the text?
options: More time for family and rest

questions.q: What challenge does the text describe?
options: The boundary between work and personal life fades

questions.q: What are hybrid models meant to do?
options: Force everyone back to the office
Combine the benefits of office and remote work

title: What Festivals Tell Us
summary: 节日如何反映文化价值，以及传统在现代的演变。
body: Festivals offer a fascinating window into the values and history of a culture. Whether it is a harvest celebration, a religious holiday, or a national day, each festival tells a story about what a community considers important. Food, music, and clothing during these events are rarely chosen at random; they often carry meanings passed down through generations. In many countries, for example, sharing a particular dish symbolises unity or good fortune for the year ahead. Interestingly, festivals also adapt as societies change. Traditions that once took place only in villages may now be celebrated in enormous cities, sometimes broadcast to millions of viewers online. Critics worry that commercial interests can hollow out the original meaning, turning sacred customs into mere entertainment. Others argue that change has always been part of tradition, and that a living festival must evolve to survive. What remains constant is the human desire to gather, to remember, and to mark the passing of time together.

questions.q: According to the text, festival food and clothing are:
What do critics worry about?
options: Commercial interests can weaken the original meaning

questions.q: What does the writer say remains constant?
options: The human desire to gather and remember

title: The Science of Sleep
summary: 睡眠并非被动休息，而是大脑最活跃的修复与记忆过程之一。
body: Sleep, once dismissed as a passive state in which the brain simply switched off, is now understood to be one of the most active and essential processes in human biology. During the night, the brain cycles repeatedly through distinct stages, each serving a different purpose. In deep sleep, the body repairs tissue and consolidates memories, transferring fragile new information into more stable long-term storage. During rapid eye movement sleep, vivid dreams unfold, and the brain appears to integrate emotional experiences and creative connections. Chronic sleep deprivation, increasingly common in modern societies, is far from harmless. Studies have linked it to impaired concentration, weakened immunity, and a heightened risk of conditions ranging from obesity to depression. Perhaps most troubling, tired individuals frequently underestimate the extent of their own impairment, mistakenly believing they have adapted to functioning on little rest. Researchers therefore caution that no amount of caffeine or willpower can fully substitute for adequate sleep. Protecting it, they argue, may be one of the simplest yet most powerful investments in long-term health.

questions.q: How was sleep viewed in the past, according to the text?
What happens during deep sleep?
options: The body repairs tissue and consolidates memories

questions.q: What does the text say about tired people?
options: They often underestimate their own impairment

title: Designing Greener Cities
summary: 城市设计如何从以汽车为中心转向以人为本、更宜居环保。
body: As the world's population becomes overwhelmingly urban, the design of cities has emerged as a decisive factor in the fight against environmental decline. For much of the twentieth century, urban planning prioritised the automobile, carving wide roads through neighbourhoods and pushing homes ever farther from workplaces. The consequences are now painfully visible: congestion, polluted air, and sprawling districts that are impossible to navigate without a car. A growing movement of planners and architects proposes a different vision. They advocate compact, walkable neighbourhoods where shops, schools, and offices sit within a short distance of one another. Green roofs, urban forests, and restored waterways are introduced not merely for beauty but to cool the air, absorb rainfall, and shelter wildlife. Critics caution that such transformations are expensive and can accelerate the displacement of poorer residents if managed carelessly. Nevertheless, the underlying principle is difficult to dispute: a city built around people rather than machines tends to be healthier, more resilient, and considerably more pleasant to inhabit.

glossary.meaning: 有韧性的，能快速恢复的

questions.q: What did twentieth-century urban planning prioritise?
Why are green roofs and urban forests introduced?
options: To cool the air, absorb rainfall, and shelter wildlife

questions.q: What concern do critics raise?
options: Transformations can be costly and displace poorer residents

title: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence
summary: 当算法主导重大决策时，责任、公平与透明成为核心难题。
body: The rapid ascent of artificial intelligence has provoked a debate that is as much philosophical as it is technological. As algorithms increasingly mediate the decisions that shape our lives, from the loans we are granted to the news we encounter, questions of accountability grow ever more urgent. When an automated system denies someone employment or misidentifies an individual as a suspect, who bears responsibility, the engineer who wrote the code, the company that deployed it, or the opaque model itself, whose reasoning even its creators struggle to interpret? Proponents contend that, handled judiciously, such systems can mitigate the inconsistencies and prejudices that plague human judgement. Yet a substantial body of research suggests the opposite risk: that models trained on historical data may not merely reproduce existing inequalities but entrench and amplify them under a veneer of mathematical objectivity. The most thoughtful commentators resist the temptation to frame the matter as a simple choice between technophilia and alarmism. They insist instead on rigorous transparency, meaningful human oversight, and legal frameworks capable of keeping pace with innovation. The central challenge, ultimately, is not whether machines can think, but whether the societies deploying them will choose to think carefully enough.

questions.q: What kind of debate does AI provoke, according to the text?
What risk does research suggest about models trained on historical data?
options: They only reproduce, never amplify, inequalities
They may entrench and amplify existing inequalities

questions.q: What do the most thoughtful commentators call for?
options: Transparency, human oversight, and legal frameworks

title: The Vanishing of Languages
summary: 语言消亡不仅失去词汇，更是失去一种感知世界的方式。
body: Every fortnight, somewhere in the world, the last fluent speaker of a language dies, and with them an entire way of perceiving reality quietly vanishes. Linguists estimate that of the roughly seven thousand languages spoken today, perhaps half will fall silent before the century is out. To the casual observer, this may seem an unfortunate but ultimately trivial consequence of globalisation, an inevitable tidying-up as commerce and communication gravitate toward a handful of dominant tongues. Such indifference, however, betrays a profound misunderstanding of what a language actually is. Far from being a mere inventory of labels for pre-existing objects, each language encodes a distinctive cosmology: subtle distinctions of colour, kinship, time, and obligation that may have no equivalent elsewhere. When a language disappears, we lose not only words but centuries of accumulated observation about medicinal plants, seasonal patterns, and social harmony. Efforts to document and revitalise endangered tongues, once dismissed as sentimental antiquarianism, are increasingly recognised as a form of intellectual conservation no less vital than the preservation of biodiversity. The impoverishment of our linguistic ecosystem, in short, diminishes the collective imagination of our entire species.

questions.q: Roughly how many of today's languages may fall silent this century?
How does the writer describe a language?
options: A distinctive cosmology encoding subtle distinctions

questions.q: How is the effort to revitalise endangered languages increasingly seen?
options: As a form of intellectual conservation
